# Fixture answers for scripts/gather-brief.sh
# System: Task Tracker — assumes requirements.md is already populated
# (gather-brief.sh detects requirements.md and skips requirements/constraints)
# Each line is one stdin read() call in the script.
# Blank lines represent pressing Enter (default / stop-loop).
# Lines beginning with # are NOT part of the fixture — they are comments for humans.
# To use: grep -v '^#' brief-answers.txt | gather-brief.sh

# §01 System Overview (multiline, end with EOF)
Task Tracker is a web application and REST API for personal task management.
Users create and organise tasks with titles, descriptions, due dates, and tags.
An email notification system sends reminders before deadlines. An admin console
allows operators to manage users and monitor system health.
EOF

# §02 Conventions (requirements.md handles constraints)
# Convention 1
REST API endpoints follow the JSON:API specification for consistent request/response structure
Reduces client implementation effort and enables generic tooling
y
# Convention 2
All database migrations use Flyway with sequential versioning
Ensures reproducible schema state across environments

# §03 Context Diagram (Mermaid, end with EOF)
flowchart TD
    User([End User]) -->|HTTPS| WebApp[Task Tracker Web App]
    Admin([System Admin]) -->|HTTPS| WebApp
    WebApp -->|REST/JSON| API[Task Tracker API]
    API -->|SMTP| Email[SendGrid Email Service]
EOF

# §03 External Actors — 2 then stop
End User
Registered individual managing their personal tasks
Creates, updates, and completes tasks; receives email reminders
y
System Administrator
Operations team member responsible for platform health
Manages user accounts, monitors health metrics, performs incident response


# §03 External Systems — 1 then stop
SendGrid
Third-party transactional email delivery service
Task reminder notifications and password reset emails


# §03 Out of Scope
Mobile native apps, billing and subscription management, team collaboration features

# §04 Technology Decisions — 3 then stop
REST API with JSON over HTTPS
Widely understood, strong client ecosystem, compatible with all target platforms
Reliability, Security
y
PostgreSQL for persistent storage
Organisational standard; strong ACID compliance, JSONB for flexible task metadata
Reliability
y
JWT for stateless authentication
Eliminates session store as a single point of failure; horizontally scalable
Reliability, Security


# §04 Architectural Approach (multiline, end with EOF)
Three-tier web application: React single-page application, Node.js/Express REST API,
PostgreSQL database. All tiers are containerised and deployed on AWS ECS Fargate.
The architecture is deliberately simple — a monolith-first approach to minimise
operational complexity at initial scale. The API is the sole integration point
for both the web app and future clients.
EOF

# §04 How strategy achieves quality goals (multiline, end with EOF)
Performance: stateless JWT auth eliminates session lookup latency; PostgreSQL connection
pooling via PgBouncer keeps query times predictable.
Reliability: ECS Fargate with health checks and auto-restart covers process failure;
RDS Multi-AZ covers database failure.
Security: TLS termination at the ALB; bcrypt password hashing; JWT with short expiry.
EOF

# §05 Container Diagram (Mermaid, end with EOF)
flowchart TD
    User([User]) -->|HTTPS| ALB[Application Load Balancer]
    ALB --> WebApp[React Web App\nECS Fargate]
    ALB --> API[Express REST API\nECS Fargate]
    API --> DB[(PostgreSQL\nRDS Multi-AZ)]
    API --> Email[SendGrid API]
EOF

# §05 Level 1 Containers — 3 then stop
React Web App
React + TypeScript, served from S3/CloudFront
Delivers the browser-based user interface
y
Express REST API
Node.js + Express, containerised on ECS Fargate
Handles all business logic and data access
y
PostgreSQL Database
AWS RDS Multi-AZ, PostgreSQL 16
Persists users, tasks, tags, and notification state


# §05 Level 2 Components — skip
# (leave Container blank to skip)


# §06 Key Scenarios — 2 then stop
Create Task
Demonstrates the primary user action and validates API → DB integration
# Sequence diagram for Create Task (Mermaid, end with EOF)
sequenceDiagram
    actor User
    participant WebApp as Web App
    participant API as REST API
    participant DB as PostgreSQL

    User->>WebApp: Fill in task form and click Save
    WebApp->>API: POST /tasks {title, description, dueDate}
    API->>DB: INSERT INTO tasks ...
    DB-->>API: task_id
    API-->>WebApp: 201 Created {taskId, ...}
    WebApp-->>User: Task appears in list
EOF
y
API returns 503 when database is unavailable
Validates graceful degradation and meaningful error responses to the client
# Sequence diagram for 503 failure case (Mermaid, end with EOF)
sequenceDiagram
    actor User
    participant WebApp as Web App
    participant API as REST API
    participant DB as PostgreSQL

    User->>WebApp: Any action requiring data
    WebApp->>API: Any authenticated request
    API->>DB: Query attempt
    DB-->>API: Connection refused
    API-->>WebApp: 503 Service Unavailable {error: "Database unavailable"}
    WebApp-->>User: Error banner: "Service temporarily unavailable. Please try again shortly."
EOF


# §07 Environments — 3 then stop
Development
Local development on developer machines
Uses Docker Compose; SQLite replaces RDS; no email sending (console output only)
y
Staging
Pre-production validation and UAT
Full AWS stack at reduced scale (single-AZ RDS); uses SendGrid sandbox mode
y
Production
Live system serving end users
Full AWS stack; RDS Multi-AZ; real SendGrid sending; CloudWatch alerting enabled


# §07 Infrastructure (multiline, end with EOF)
AWS us-east-1. ECS Fargate for compute (no EC2 management). RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ
for the database. Application Load Balancer for TLS termination and routing.
S3 + CloudFront for the React SPA static assets. ECR for container images.
Route 53 for DNS. ACM for TLS certificates.
Infrastructure defined in Terraform, stored in the same monorepo under infra/.
EOF

# §07 Deployment Process (multiline, end with EOF)
Trigger: merge to main branch.
Pipeline: GitHub Actions — lint -> unit tests -> build Docker image -> push to ECR ->
deploy to ECS Fargate (rolling update, 100% minimum healthy).
Configuration: environment variables injected at runtime from AWS Secrets Manager
(Twelve-Factor Factor III — config in the environment).
Approval gate: none for staging; production deployment requires one manual approval
in the GitHub Actions workflow.
Rollback: ECS keeps the previous task definition; rollback is a one-command ECS
update to the previous revision.
EOF

# §08 Security (multiline, end with EOF)
Authentication: JWT Bearer tokens (15-minute access token + 7-day refresh token).
Authorisation: ownership-based — users can only read/write their own tasks; admin
role enforced via JWT claim checked on every admin endpoint.
Data protection: bcrypt (cost factor 12) for passwords; TLS 1.3 in transit;
RDS encryption at rest using AWS KMS.
OWASP Top 10 mitigations: parameterised queries (A03 Injection); Content-Security-Policy
header (A03 XSS); rate limiting on auth endpoints (A04 Insecure Design).
EOF

# §08 Observability (multiline, end with EOF)
Logging: structured JSON logs (Twelve-Factor XI), shipped to CloudWatch Logs.
Log levels: ERROR for exceptions, WARN for recoverable failures, INFO for business events.
Metrics: CloudWatch custom metrics for API latency (p50/p95/p99), error rate,
active users. Collected via the CloudWatch agent on ECS.
Tracing: AWS X-Ray for distributed tracing on the API service.
Alerting: CloudWatch Alarms — p95 latency >500ms, error rate >1%, RDS CPU >80%.
PagerDuty integration for on-call escalation.
EOF

# §08 Error Handling (multiline, end with EOF)
Errors returned as JSON {error: string, code: string} with appropriate HTTP status codes.
Retries: exponential backoff with jitter for SendGrid calls (max 3 attempts).
Circuit breaker: if SendGrid fails 3 consecutive times, email sending is disabled
for 60 seconds and tasks are queued for retry.
User-facing messages: generic "Something went wrong" for 5xx errors; specific
validation messages for 4xx errors.
EOF

# §08 Domain Model (multiline, end with EOF)
User: a registered account. Used by: API (auth, task ownership), Admin Console.
Task: a unit of work with title, description, due date, status, and tags.
  Core entity — used by all containers.
Tag: a label that can be applied to multiple tasks for organisation.
  Used by: API, Web App.
Notification: a scheduled email reminder linked to a task's due date.
  Used by: API, notification worker.
EOF

# §09 ADRs — 2 then stop
Use JWT for stateless authentication
# Context (multiline, end with EOF)
We need user authentication. Options considered: server-side sessions (Redis),
JWT tokens, OAuth2 with an external provider. The system has no existing session
store and must be horizontally scalable without sticky sessions.
EOF
We will use JWT Bearer tokens with a 15-minute access token lifetime and
7-day refresh tokens stored in httpOnly cookies.
# Positive consequences (multiline, end with EOF)
Stateless — any API instance can validate a token without shared state.
No session store dependency — eliminates a component.
EOF
# Negative consequences (multiline, end with EOF)
Tokens cannot be invalidated before expiry without a blocklist.
Short access token lifetime means frequent refresh requests.
EOF
y
Use PostgreSQL as the primary data store
# Context (multiline, end with EOF)
We need persistent storage for users, tasks, and related data. Options: PostgreSQL,
MySQL, MongoDB, DynamoDB. The data is relational (users own tasks, tasks have tags).
The organisation mandates a supported database with existing DBA expertise.
EOF
We will use PostgreSQL 16 on AWS RDS Multi-AZ as the sole persistent data store.
# Positive consequences (multiline, end with EOF)
Strong ACID compliance for task state transitions.
JSONB support for flexible task metadata without schema changes.
Organisational support and DBA expertise available.
EOF
# Negative consequences (multiline, end with EOF)
Single database is a scalability ceiling at high write volumes.
RDS Multi-AZ adds cost compared to single-instance.
EOF


# §10 Quality Tree — 3 then stop
API Response Time
QS-01
High
y
System Availability
QS-02
High
y
Data Security
QS-03
High


# §10 Quality Scenarios — 3 then stop
QS-01
API Response Time
100 concurrent users submit authenticated API requests
Normal load, production environment
API processes each request and returns a response
p95 response time <=300ms measured by k6 load test
y
QS-02
System Availability
Scheduled maintenance or unexpected process failure occurs
Production environment, any time of day
System recovers automatically without data loss
99.5% uptime over any rolling 30-day window; RTO <=5 minutes
y
QS-03
Data Security
Unauthenticated request attempts to access another user's task
Production environment, normal operation
API rejects the request
HTTP 401 or 403 returned; no task data disclosed; incident logged


# §11 Risks — 2 then stop
SendGrid service outage disrupts email notifications and password reset
M
H
Queue email notifications locally; provide in-app notification as fallback; document manual password reset procedure for ops
y
PostgreSQL RDS failure causes full service outage
L
H
RDS Multi-AZ provides automatic failover; RTO <=5 minutes; daily automated snapshots for RPO <=24 hours


# §11 Technical Debt — 1 then stop
No integration test suite — only unit tests exist at launch
Prudent x Deliberate
Add integration test suite covering the 5 highest-risk API endpoints in Q4 2026; tracked in backlog
